exemplar.title: The aim of this study is to investigate the impact of time for reading a passage and answering questions (2 or 5 minutes) on a comprehension test score (%).

detailedExemplarData.title: The aim of this study is to investigate the impact of time for reading a passage and answering questions (2 or 5 minutes) on a comprehension test score (%).

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and fully operationalised aim with explicit IV (time limits) and DV (percentage correct)
Relevance explained in depth, linking timing to educational assessment validity and student well-being
Dual-process model accurately described and explicitly connected to timing conditions
Hypotheses include both null and research forms with fully operationalised variables
Consistent, precise terminology throughout the introduction
weaknesses: Minor phrasing lapses in one linkage sentence left slight truncation
Citation of the dual-process model lacks full publication details
Could explicitly predict direction of effect to sharpen the aim
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although minor phrasing and citation details are incomplete, the student fully meets all top-band descriptors: the aim is clear and its relevance is thoroughly explained; the dual-process theory is described and linked; and the hypotheses are operationalised. This warrants the maximum mark of 6.

strengths: Research design choice is explained with reference to order effects and ecological validity
Convenience sampling is justified in context and limitations noted
Controlled variables and materials are listed and each control is explained
Limitations of sampling and materials are acknowledged
weaknesses: One control (font specification) is marginal for reproducibility
Material labels could be clearer for replication
Discussion could extend to alternative designs (e.g., counterbalancing)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Exploration meet the top band: the design is not only described but explained; sampling choices are justified; and controls are justified. Despite minor clarity issues, the criterion is fully satisfied, meriting 4/4.

strengths: Appropriate inferential (Mann-Whitney U) and descriptive (median, IQR) statistics are accurately applied
Statistical analysis is correctly chosen for non-parametric data and properly executed
Findings are interpreted with respect to data and directly linked to hypotheses
Graphical presentation addresses the hypothesis and correctly represents the data
weaknesses: Graph axes lack full unit labels and error bars for precision
Table headings are not as detailed as recommended
Minor terminological slip in describing data scale
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite small errors in labelling and terminology, the student applies both descriptive and inferential statistics accurately, presents a correct graph, and interprets findings in light of the hypothesis. All top-band descriptors are met, justifying the full 6 marks.

strengths: Findings are discussed with clear reference to the dual-process theory
Strengths and limitations are stated, explained, and directly linked to validity and generalisability
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and fully justified
Discussion shows coherent integration of results with theoretical framework
weaknesses: Additional citations would strengthen theoretical and statistical foundations
Discussion of alternative explanations (e.g., motivation or anxiety) could deepen analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student meets all criteria for the top band: findings are discussed in theory context; strengths, limitations, and modifications are explained and justified. Minor expansions are possible, but the criterion descriptors are fully satisfied, warranting 6/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart of median scores directly addresses the hypothesis, but **axis labels are minimal and units are omitted**, limiting clarity.  Therefore, the graph meets the 3-4 band but with noticeable errors, meriting 3.

The student **states statistical findings, interprets their meaning in relation to the data, and clearly links them to acceptance/rejection of the hypotheses and to the effect of time on scores**.  Interpretation is coherent, though depth could be greater, so 5 in the top band is suitable.

Both research and null hypotheses are given and include **operationalised IV (2 min vs 5 min time limit) and DV (percentage correct on an 11-item test)**.  Operationalisation is explicit; wording is precise enough, so the strand meets the 5-6 band.  Minor wording inconsistencies keep the score at 5.

Convenience sampling is described and the choice is **explained in terms of practicality and relevance to high-school context**.  Limitations concerning representativeness are noted.  The explanation is adequate but relatively brief, warranting 3 within the 3-4 band.

The dual-process model is **accurately described** (System 1 vs System 2 with speed, automaticity, bias, etc.) and the student **explicitly explains how the two timing conditions are intended to elicit the two systems**, thereby connecting theory to the investigation.  Description and linkage are sufficiently clear though not deeply nuanced, meriting 5 in the top band.

Findings are **described with reference to the dual-process theory**, but the discussion remains surface-level, lacking critical depth about alternative explanations.  This satisfies the 3-4 descriptor; clarity justifies the upper mark of 4.

The student clearly states the aim â to investigate whether 2 min or 5 min reading/answering time affects comprehension scores â and then **explains why this matters for high-school testing practice and accurate assessment of learning**.  The explanation links time pressure to potential errors and student well-being, showing more than a simple identification of relevance.  Minor phrasing lapses remain, so a solid 5 (not 6) within the top band is appropriate.

Multiple strengths and limitations related to design, procedure, and sample are **stated and explained in terms of their impact on validity and generalisability**, fulfilling the 5-6 band.  Some explanations are brief, hence 5 rather than 6.

Several controlled variables (room, materials, instructions) are listed and **justifications are given for why each control is needed**.  Some phrasing is repetitive and one or two controls (font) are marginal, but overall the choice of materials and controls is explainedâearning a mid-band score of 3.

Two concrete modifications (adjust timing; broaden sample) are **explicitly linked back to the identified limitations and justified as ways to improve validity and generalisability**.  While succinct, the justification is clear, supporting a score of 5 in the top band.

The student not only describes that an independent-samples design was used but **explains why it was chosen (avoiding order effects and demand characteristics)** and why repeated measures was rejected.  This explanatory element meets the 3-4 band; coverage is thorough, so the top of the band (4) is justified.

Appropriate descriptive statistics (median, IQR) and an inferential test (Mann-Whitney U) are applied correctly for two independent, non-parametric samples.  Calculations match raw data and the critical comparison is correct.  Minor terminological slips (calling scores âratioâ) prevent a perfect 6, so 5 is awarded.

annotations.comment:
The hypotheses are clear but could link predictions explicitly to dual-process theory expectations (e.g.
System 2 leads to fewer errors).
The choice of an independent-samples design is well explained, including avoidance of order effects, strengthening internal validity.

Discussion of independent samples could expand on alternative designs (e.g.
counterbalancing) to show deeper methodological consideration.
Convenience sampling is justified by practicality and ecological validity, though note its impact on representativeness more explicitly.
The aim succinctly identifies the IV (2 min vs 5 min) and DV (% correct), showing effective operationalisation, though explicitly stating the expected direction would sharpen focus.
The relevance discussion robustly links test timing to learning outcomes and student well-being, demonstrating clear awareness of educational impact.
The dual-process model is well described, but citation detail is minimal; add page numbers or expand on Kahnemanâs criteria for System 1 vs System 2.
The linkage sentence is truncated, leaving the rationale incomplete; ensure full elaboration of how timing elicits System 1 vs System 2.
Variables are correctly identified and operationalised with time limits and % scores; consider noting potential confounders like reading ability.

Table 1 lacks clear column headings and a detailed caption; label columns (e.g.
âConditionâ, âMedian (%)â, âIQRâ) for reader clarity.

The bar chart should include axis labels (e.g.
âMedian Score (%)â) and error bars; without units, the visual loses precision.

Quantify sample diversity (e.g.
percentage by cultural background) to substantiate claims of representativeness.
The modification proposal is truncated; fully justify each suggested change by linking directly to identified limitations.
Only one reference is cited; integrate additional sources on cognitive theory and nonparametric analysis to strengthen the theoretical and statistical foundation.
The Kahneman citation lacks full publication details and adherence to IB formatting; add publisher location and consistent style.
Sampling limitations are noted, but strengthen by proposing stratified or random sampling to mitigate IB-only bias.

Material names like â$\mathbf{L}^{\text{max}}$ Timerâ are unclear; use precise labels (e.g.
âdigital countdown timerâ) for reproducibility.
Controlling environmental factors is well justified; consider a pretest reading baseline to further isolate timing effects.
Consistency of materials (same story/booklet) is clearly stated, ensuring the IV remains the only manipulation.
Single-blinding is good, but adopting double-blind (tester uninformed) would further reduce experimenter bias.
Median and IQR are appropriate, but clarify that score data are ordinal or ratio to justify nonparametric choice.
Interpretation effectively links median differences to system engagement, reinforcing hypothesis alignment.
Report the exact U statistic, p-value, and how ties were handled to improve statistical transparency.

Discussion ties findings to dualâprocess theory well, but could explore alternative explanations (e.g.
motivation, test anxiety).
Combine strengths and limitations into clearly labeled subsections, and directly connect each limitation to its impact on the hypothesis.
